coredata.dc:title: A QCM humidity sensor constructed by graphene quantum dots and chitosan composites
coredata.dc:creator: Qi, Pengjia
Zhang, Tong
Shao, Jieren
Yang, Bai
Fei, Teng
Wang, Rui
coredata.dc:description: A quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) humidity sensor based on graphene quantum dots-chitosan (GQDs-CS) sensitive film has been successfully fabricated in this work. The composite ratio of sensitive material and the coating quality of the sensitive film were optimized. The morphological and chemical properties of GQDs-CS composites were inspected via scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The results manifest that prepared QCM sensor exhibits high response sensitivity (a frequency shift of −3291 Hz at 95% RH), short response/recovery time (36 s/3 s), tiny humidity hysteresis (˜1.6% RH), and excellent reproducibility, reversibility and long-term stability. Additionally, the electroacoustic parameters of the QCM sensors were also investigated by means of the impedance analysis method. Based on dynamic and static analysis of the sensor, it is speculated that the introduction of GQDs into CS enhanced the mechanical stiffness of the CS film, which could effectively improve the performance of QCM sensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quartz crystal microbalance
Humidity sensor
Graphene quantum dots
Chitosan

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and measurement of QCM humidity sensors
The optimization of QCM humidity sensor
Impedance analysis of the QCM humidity sensors
The humidity sensing characteristics of prepared QCM-b sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The device structure of (a) QCM and (b) schematic illustration of film deposition by dropping method.
Measurement system of the QCM sensor for humidity detection.
FT-IR spectra of CS, GQDs and GQDs-CS composites.

The SEM images of (a) CS and (b) GQDs-CS composites. The (c) TEM and (d) HRTEM images of GQDs (the corresponding size distribution is inserted in (c)). The (e) TEM and (f) HRTEM images of GQDs-CS composites.
The relationships between the frequency shift of QCM-1 to QCM-6 and the corresponding relative humidity. The inset shows the linear fitting curves of the Log (Δf) versus humidity for QCM-1 to QCM-6.
The structural schematic diagram of the GQDs-CS composites.
The relationships between the frequency shift of QCM-a to QCM-e and the corresponding relative humidity. The inset shows the linear fitting curves of the Log (Δf) versus humidity for QCM-a to QCM-e.

The conductance spectra of the QCM sensors with different sensing films at various humidities (a) CS; (b) GQDs; (c) GQDs-CS. (d) The relationship between Q-factor (D-factor) and relative humidity with different sensing films.
(a) The frequency response curves of QCM-b from low to high humidity (11% RH-95% RH); (b) The change of frequency shift and mass in different humidities.

(a) The response and recovery time of QCM-b at the range from 11% RH to 95% RH. (b)The repeatability response curve of QCM-b at the range from 11% RH to 95% RH for five cycles. (c) Humidity hysteresis characteristic of the QCM-b. (d) The long-term stability of QCM-b.
Selectivity of QCM-b at room temperature.
List of QCM information before and after GQDs-CS film deposition (with same drop quantity but different mass ratio materials).
List of QCM information before and after GQDs-CS film deposition (with same material but different drop quantity).
List of the Q-factor and D-factor of different sensing materials at various humidities.
Comparison between sensor in this work and reported work.

head.ce:title: A QCM humidity sensor constructed by graphene quantum dots and chitosan composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics, College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics
College of Electronic Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) humidity sensor based on graphene quantum dots-chitosan (GQDs-CS) sensitive film has been successfully fabricated in this work. The composite ratio of sensitive material and the coating quality of the sensitive film were optimized. The morphological and chemical properties of GQDs-CS composites were inspected via scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The results manifest that prepared QCM sensor exhibits high response sensitivity (a frequency shift of −3291 Hz at 95% RH), short response/recovery time (36 s/3 s), tiny humidity hysteresis (˜1.6% RH), and excellent reproducibility, reversibility and long-term stability. Additionally, the electroacoustic parameters of the QCM sensors were also investigated by means of the impedance analysis method. Based on dynamic and static analysis of the sensor, it is speculated that the introduction of GQDs into CS enhanced the mechanical stiffness of the CS film, which could effectively improve the performance of QCM sensor.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, human survival and social activities are closely related to humidity. The technical requirements of humidity sensors for different application are also different. From the perspective of manufacturing, the performance and technical index of humidity sensors can vary greatly on materials, structures and processes. For instance, multiple sensing technologies including acoustic [1–3], resistance [4,5], optical [6–8], impedance [9], capacitance [10], thermal [11], and resonance [12], etc. have been explored to monitor the humidity. Quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) is a type of technology that uses frequency as a detection signal. It is worth mentioning that QCM humidity sensors always work at room temperature, possess a wide range of measurements, rapid response-recovery velocity and low detection limits of nanogram level [13–17]. QCM sensors have been used to detect the mass change via measuring the frequency shift due to the adsorption of molecules on the electrode surface which coated with sensing material.

Sensitive material is the key of a QCM sensor. Various hydrophilic materials such as polymers [16,18], metal oxides [19–21], carbon based materials [22,23], etc. were used to detect moisture. Among above materials, graphene has drew considerable focus owing to their remarkable mechanical flexibility, high carrier mobility, good thermal chemical stability and numerous chemically active defect sites [24–26]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can be seen as part of graphene, which own graphene structure inside and possess a smaller size and a certain amount of oxygen atoms in the form of functional groups, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl [27]. However, GQDs lack chiral active site, easy dissolution, instability on the electrode surface, etc., which limit their application in the field of sensors [28]. Therefore, in order to further improve the sensitivity, selectivity and affinity for target analytes, additional modifications to GQDs are required. Chitosan (CS) is a kind of natural polysaccharide which contains plentiful functional groups such as hydroxyl and amine. It possesses hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, chemical inertness and great film forming ability [29–31]. However, it has poor conductivity, inferior mechanical performance and low detection signal at the surface of the electrode, so it cannot be widely used in the field of transducer. The combination of GQDs with CS can improve the apparent morphology of CS, and that GQDs can be well dispersed in the matrix of CS [28]. Therefore, it is important to explore the preparation, characterization and application of GQDs and CS composites.

In this work, we aimed to fabricate a highly sensitive, fast response-recovery, tiny hysteresis and higher-quality QCM humidity sensor with GQDs-CS sensing film and further investigated the sensing mechanism of the sensor.

All the chemical reagents used in the experiment were analytical grade. Graphite powder, HNO3 (65%), H2SO4 (98%), Na2CO3, Ethylenediamine (EDA) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). CS (80%˜95% deacetylation), acetic acid (36%), ethanol and acetone were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were used without any further purification. Deionized water was used in all experiments.

GQDs were prepared through acid cutting and post EDA treatment. 300 mg graphite powder was dispersed in the mixed acid (containing concentrated H2SO4 60 mL and concentrated HNO3 20 mL). The solution was then poured into a 150 mL round-bottomed flask, and stirred at 120 °C for 10 h. After the reaction, the solution was diluted by putting it into 300 mL deionized water, followed by neutralizing the acid with abundant Na2CO3. After that, the solution was concentrated by using rotary evaporator and put into the refrigerator to exclude the extra Na2SO4 and NaNO3 salt from the solution as much as possible (this process was repeated three times). Extra Inorganic salt in the solution was then removed using a filter membrane of 220 nm. Then a 3500 dialysis bag was used for 5 days to further purify the sample. 9 mL EDA was added to the 2 mL GQD aqueous solution (0.4 mg/mL) and this was subjected to ultrasonication for 10 min. Then the mixed solution was put into a 30 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. By using rotary evaporator to remove the extra solvent and dispersing it in 10 mL deionized water, the obtained product is collected. Finally, a 500–1000 dialysis bag was used for 5 days to further purify the sample, and then the GQDs material was obtained.

An aqueous CS solution (5 mg/mL) was prepared by dissolving 0.25 g CS powder into 50 mL of acetic acid buffer (0.05 M) solution followed by stirring overnight at room temperature. The amine groups of the CS solution will be protonated during this period. Next step, the GQDs solution and the CS solution were mixed with vigorous stirring. Finally, the homogeneous GQDs-CS dispersion was ultrasound dispersed evenly after two hours.

The FT-IR spectra of GQDs-CS were obtained on a WQF-510AFTIR spectrometer, using KBr pellets as the reference. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were taken by a JSM-6700 F electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HTEM) was recorded on a JEOL JEM-2100 F. DLS was performed using a Zetasizer Nano-ZS (Malvern Instruments). Each sample was measured 5 times and the average data was presented.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and measurement of QCM humidity sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The QCM substrates used in this work were purchased from Beijing Chenjing Electronic Co., Ltd. China. The QCM was consisted of AT-cut quartz crystal (8 mm diameter) with a fundamental frequency of 10 MHz and silver electrodes (5 mm diameter) were covered on both sides, the electrode leaded two pins for testing purposes (Fig. 1 (a)). As a typical procedure, the QCM substrates were ultrasonic cleaned by acetone, ethanol and deionized water for 10 min successively, and then dried in an air-tight container which filled with inert nitrogen gas for the whole night at room temperature. The QCM humidity sensor was obtained via dropping the as-prepared GQDs-CS composites dispersion by micropipette on the Ag-electrodes surface of the QCM (Fig. 1(b)). Then, the modified QCM sensor was put into the foregoing container for at least 12 h. In this study, a calculated amount of composites with different mass ratio of GQDs to CS (1.0:1.0, 1.0:1.2, 1.0:1.5, 1.0:1.7, 1.0:2.0, 1.0:2.2) was stirred for 6 h and set aside for use.

Fig. 2 shows the schematic diagram of the QCM sensor for humidity detection. A self-made oscillator circuit was utilized to drive the coated QCM sensor, and then exposed to the supersaturated salt solution of LiCl, MgCl2, Mg(NO3), NaCl, KCl, KNO3 to build a relative humidity level ambient of 11%, 33%, 54%, 75%, 85% and 95%, respectively. The dynamic response characteristics were monitored by a frequency meter (53131 A, Agilent) and varying resonance frequencies were collected by computer in real-time. The electroacoustic parameters of the QCM sensor were analyzed by a precision impedance analyzer (4294 A, Agilent) and the admittance spectrum, resonant frequency, and Q-factor were obtained in the meantime. Additionally, the 11% RH was chosen as a standard humidity environment to measure the oscillation frequency of the QCM sensor and then the relative humidity was changed to other humidity environment. Meanwhile, a new set of oscillation frequency was measured. Finally the humidity was switched to 11% RH for recovery. Point out emphatically, all the humidity sensing experiments were implemented at room temperature (25 °C) without any exceptional operation.

FT-IR spectra were used to identify the surface functional groups present on CS, GQDs and GQDs-CS. As shown in Fig. 3 , the spectrum of CS shows a broad band at 3411.5 cm−1, which is the multiple absorption peak of OH stretching vibration absorption peak overlapped with NH stretching vibration absorption peak. Two characteristic peaks at 2929.3 cm−1 and 2875.3 cm−1 can be assigned to C–H stretching vibration respectively. The NH bending and deformation vibration of amine groups appears at around 1571.7 cm−1. Other major characteristic peaks in CS are observed at 1415.5 cm−1 (C–N stretching vibration), 1153.2 cm−1 (anti-symmetrical stretching vibration of the COC bridge), 1087.8 cm−1 (variable angular vibration absorption peak of alcohol-hydroxyl group) and 898.6 cm−1 (characteristic absorption peak of glycoside bonds) [30,32]. For GQDs, the broad absorption bands at around 3405.7 cm−1 can be assigned to stretching vibrations of OH and NH, which indicate that there are lots of amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of GQDs. The peak at 1664.2 cm−1 is attributed to the vibrational absorption band of CO in COOH and CONH. Additional peaks in GQDs are observed at 1384.6 cm−1 (C–H vibration peak), 1116.6 cm−1 (C–O stretching vibration) [33]. The spectrum of GQDs-CS presents almost all the significant characteristic bands of both GQDs and CS with some shifts in bands position. In particular, it is important to note the decreased intensities and downshift of peaks at 1571.7 cm−1 (from CS spectrum) and 1664.2 cm−1 (from GQDs spectrum), respectively to 1555.9 cm−1 and 1648.5 cm−1. This indicates there is a hydrogen bond effect between GQDs and CS [34].

The surface morphologies of the CS and GQDs-CS film were scanned by SEM measurements. Fig. 4 (a) shows the SEM of CS after acid treatment. As seen, the CS film shows a flat and relatively homogeneous surface. In Fig. 4(b), GQDs-CS presents a cellular and highly porous random three-dimensional network structure. The SEM diagram shows that GQDs can change the morphology of polymer matrix significantly by dispersing in CS as nanofiller. Fig. 4(c–f) are the TEM diagrams of GQDs and GQDs-CS. Fig. 4(c) shows that the obtained GQDs are particles with uniform size. According to particle size analysis, the average particle size of GQDs is 11.24 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image shows that the GQDs have clear crystalline lattice fringes, and the GQDs are larger scale quantum dot formed by cross-linking multiple quantum dots (Fig. 4(d)). The 0.21 nm spacing could be attributed to the (100) plane of graphene [35–38]. For the GQDs-CS composites (Fig. 4(e)), the GQDs nanofillers (black dots) are dispersed within the CS matrix and without any apparent reunion. Further observation shows that similar crystalline lattice fringes can still be seen in the composite material system (Fig. 4(f)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optimization of QCM humidity sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Six samples (1.0:1.0, 1.0:1.2, 1.0:1.5, 1.0:1.7, 1.0:2.0, 1.0:2.2) were dropped on the electrode of the QCM sensors with 1 μL and named as QCM-1, QCM-2, QCM-3, QCM-4, QCM-5 and QCM-6, respectively. In order to research the drop quantity of GQDs-CS composites on the electrode of QCM sensors, the vibration frequency before and after film deposition were tested, and results as shown in Table 1 . The deposition mass of GQDs-CS composites on the electrode of QCM sensors were calculated on the basis of the Sauerbrey equation [39]. Fig. 5 shows the curves of frequency response for all the sensors under different humidities, and the linear fitting curves of the Log (Δf) versus humidity displays inset of Fig. 5. The frequency shifts of the sensors all exhibit an acceptable logarithmic linear relationship with relative humidity. A structural schematic diagram of the GQDs-CS is given in Fig. 6 . GQDs with negative charge due to the presence of more oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface such as carboxyl and hydroxyl [40,41]. While hydrophilic CS has rich -NH2 on the flexible chain, and it is easy to be protonated in acidic medium (such as acetic acid, hydrochloric acid, etc.) [42,43]. So, there is electrostatic effect between the protonated CS and the GQDs which surface is electronegative [44]. A logical to suppose that the appropriate mass proportion of GQDs and CS could make the charge balance between two materials, and then form a more homogeneous and compact sensitive membrane. As a result, QCM-2 that owns the most suitable composite ratio of materials exhibits the highest regression coefficient which is 0.99641.

After determining the optimum quality ratio of GQDs-CS composites, the influence of drop coating quality was also discussed. The GQDs-CS composites (1.0:1.2) were dropped on the electrode of the QCM substrates with 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5 μL and named as QCM-a, QCM-b, QCM-c, QCM-d and QCM-e, respectively. As seen in Fig. 7 , the frequency shift of the QCM sensors enhances with the increasing sensitive material because more sensitive material can absorb more water molecules. But the linearity of the sensor is not consistent with the above rules, because too much or too little coating quality will affect the stability of QCM for varying degrees. As can be found in Table 2 , the regression coefficient first increases and then decreases with the increasing total mass of the sensitive material. When the total mass of composite film is too massive to QCM, the circuit can even stop oscillating. As a result, QCM-b is the sensor for all the subsequent experiments which owns the most suitable composite ratio of material (1.0:1.2) and drop coating quality (1 μL).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impedance analysis of the QCM humidity sensors
ce:section.ce:para:
The QCM can be usually modeled by a Butterworth-van-Dyke (BVD) equivalent circuit and after sensing film modified the circuit can be seen as a modified Butterworth-van-Dyke (mBVD) equivalent circuit. The conductance spectrum is frequently used to describe the resonance behaviors of QCM [14,45,46]. Several resonance parameters of QCM can be obtained in the conductance spectrum by means of the 4294 A impedance analyzer. For example, the frequency which relative to the peak value of conductance is the serial resonant frequency. The Q-factor is often defined as the ratio of the resonance frequency (f 0) to the half-peak width (HBW) of the conductance as shown in the Eq. (1). The inverse of the Q-factor is defined as d-factor, it can explain the energy dissipation of QCM during vibration.
(1)
Q =
f 0
H B W
=
D
− 1

Fig. 8 (a–c) give the conductance spectra of the QCM sensors with different sensing films at various humidities. The static humidity sensing performance was measured when the resonance reached steady state at a given humidity. It can be found that the conductance peak of each QCM sensor moves from a higher frequency to a lower frequency with increasing humidity, and the HBW becomes wider at the same time. It is worth mentioning that the dissipation of the CS modified sensor was larger than that of the GQDs as shown in Fig. 8(d). Futhermore, the Q-factor and D-factor of different sensing materials at various humidities were listed in Table 3 . The results indicate that after the introduction of GQDs into CS, the dissipation of GQDs-CS modified QCM sensor has been improved than the CS modified one which means that the Q-factor of QCM sensor has been partly enhanced. This is attributed to enhanced mechanical strength of the CS by GQDs which own a rigid structure and have the proper molecular scale and good dispersibility within CS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The humidity sensing characteristics of prepared QCM-b sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The response curve of QCM-b under full humidity range is given in Fig. 9 (a). Additionally, Fig. 9(b) describes the change of frequency shift and mass in different humidities. More and more water molecules were attached to the surface of the QCM-b with the increasing moisture. This makes the QCM-b heavier so the oscillation frequency gradually decrease and lead to the frequency shift become bigger.

The response and recovery time is crucial characteristic for estimating the property of the humidity sensors. The time taken by the sensor to achieve 90% of the total frequency change is usually defined as the response time in the case of adsorption or the recovery time in the case of desorption [47]. Fig. 10 (a) shows the response and recovery time curve of QCM-b sensor in different humidity levels with the range from 11% RH to 95% RH. The response and recovery time is about 36 s and 3 s which indicates a very fast adsorption and desorption speed of water molecules.

Via repeating the above operation five times, the repeatability response curve of QCM-b was obtained as shown in Fig. 10(b). For comparison, the repeatability of the chitosan film modified QCM sensor was shown in Fig. S1(in the Supporting information). Since the QCM based sensor works in vibration mode, the stronger mechanical properties of the film coated on the QCM substrate, the more synchronous vibration between the film and the substrate. Chitosan is a viscoelastic polymer, so it under the external force action (oscillation) it shows a strong viscosity, so the vibration of chitosan film can not keep in step with the QCM substrate, and result in frequency offset and reduced repeatability. But the QCM-b keep up with a similar oscillation amplitude during the five cycles measurement, which indicates that GQDs-CS thin film coated on surface of QCM displays excellent reproducibility and reversibility.

Humidity hysteresis is another indispensable parameter to evaluate the reliability of a humidity sensor. As a rule, humidity hysteresis is defined as the non-coincidence of the response curve of humidity sensor in the process of humidification and dehumidification [48]. The QCM-b underwent relative humidity environment variation from 11% RH to 95% RH for water adsorption, and then from 95% RH to 11% RH for water desorption. Fig. 10(c) gives the humidity hysteresis characteristic of the QCM-b. The maximum humidity hysteresis is around 1.6% RH under 85% RH for this sensor. It is quite obvious that the QCM-b displays a narrow hysteresis loop during the cyclic humidity detection, indicating a high reliability of the sensor. The hysteresis curve of QCM sensor modified by chitosan was given by the same method. As depicted in Fig. S2(in the Supporting information), pure chitosan modified QCM sensor displayed large hysteresis phenomenon at high humidity. It made the sensor less reliable and it also proved the necessity of introducing GQDs.

The long-term stability of QCM-b at different humidity levels was also been explored. The QCM-b was exposed at the humidity level from 11% RH to 95% RH every 5 days for 25 days, meanwhile the oscillation frequency was measured. As shown in Fig. 10(d), there is an acceptable slight fluctuation of frequency shifts, providing the good long-term stability and high quality of the QCM-b. Fig. 11 shows the response of QCM-b sensor to humidity (11% and 75% RH) and various gases (500 ppm) at room temperature. It can be seen that the QCM-b had a higher sensitivity towards water vapor compared to other interference gases, and only a little sense to ehtanol, formaldehyde, ammonia and acetone vapors. The performance of the QCM humidity sensor in this work and reported work are summarized in Table 4 for comparison.

In conclusion, an excellent QCM humidity sensor coated with GQDs-CS sensing film was obtained to detect full RH range. The oscillating circuit and impedance analysis methods have been adopted to examine the dynamic response and recovery behaviors and static humidity sensing performances, respectively. GQDs as nanometer filler can obtain good dispersibility in CS matrix so as to improve the mechanical properties of composite materials. In addition, the intrinsic hydrophilicity both of GQDs and CS are able to provide more water adsorption sites. The optimized QCM-b sensor demonstrated the highest humidity sensitivity which reached -3291 Hz at 95% RH, rapid response and recovery time (36 s/3 s @95%), ideal reproducibility, reversibility and long-term stability, and tiny humidity hysteresis (around 1.6% RH). The prepared QCM sensor is reliable for monitoring water vapor content in wide humidity range.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation Committee [Granted No. 61674066 and No. 61773178, and No. 51433003].

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Pengjia Qi received her BS degree from the College of Science, Changchun University of Science and Technology, China in 2014. She received her MS degree from the College of Engineering, Yanbian University, China in 2016. During MS course, she took part in the quartz crystal microbalance sensors for gas detection. She entered the DS course in 2018. Now focuses on the preparation of quartz crystal microbalance sensors based on natural polymer materials.

Tong Zhang completed her MS degree in semiconductor materials in 1992 and her PhD in the field of microelectronics and solid-state electronics in 2001 from Jilin University. She was appointed as a full-time professor in the College of Electronics Science and Engineering, Jilin University in 2001. Her research interests are sensing functional materials, gas sensors, humidity sensors and electrochemical sensors.

Jieren Shao received her BS, MS degree from Jilin University in 2015 and 2018, respectively. She is a student in the State Key Lab of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, under the direction of Prof. Bai Yang. She is currently conducting research in the synthesis methods, property and application of graphene based the carbon nanomaterial.

Bai Yang is a professor at the State Key Lab of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University. He has authored more than 430 publications (with 11,500 citations). His research was awarded the Second Prize of the State Natural Science Award of China. His current research interests are focused on polymeric carbon nanodots, polymeric nanohybridization and photoelectric materials/devices via aqueous approaches, high performance polymeric hybrid optical materials and ordered fabrication of polymeric microstructures with multi-scale and photonic responsive materials.

Teng Fei received his BS degree in 2005 in chemical engineering and technology and Ph.D. degree in 2010 in polymer chemistry and physics from Jilin University, China. He is currently an associate professor in the College of Electronics Science and Engineering, Jilin University. His research interests include sensing functional materials and devices.

Rui Wang received her BS, MS and PhD degree from the College of Electronics Science and Engineering, Jilin University, China in 2005, 2007 and 2010 respectively. She is majored in microelectronics and solid state electronics, and as a researcher engaged in novel sensing materials in Jilin University since 2010. Her current research is focused on the low-dimensional materials, carbon-based functional materials and chemical sensors.
